title: The Wrapper Illusion: Do Entity Structures Neutralize Tax Anti-Abuse Rules?
gist: AQR argues that wrapping trades in an LP or LLC (especially a fund-of-one) does NOT reliably bypass the three U.S. tax anti-abuse regimes — wash sales (1091), straddles (1092), and constructive sales (1259) — because the tax code follows economic substance, not legal form. The defensibility of a wrapper scales with genuine economic separation: SMAs/single-member LLCs are disregarded entirely, funds-of-one are the most dangerous illusion, and only genuine commingled funds get partial protection (and only for wash sales, never straddles).

insights.claim: U.S. tax law follows economic substance rather than legal form, so any strategy relying on an entity wrapper to achieve an outcome unsupported by the underlying economics carries meaningful tax, compliance, and reputational risk.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Allocators using LP/LLC structures for tax positioning should not assume the wrapper insulates them; the IRS can disregard form-only structures.
insights.quote: The tax law has evolved to follow economic substance, not legal form.
insights.locator: Answering this question requires an understanding

insights.claim: The threshold test for any wrapper is whether it has a pre-tax investment rationale; if its only purpose is to alter a tax outcome, AQR says the analysis should end there and the structure should be avoided.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: A clean screen for evaluating tax structures: no standalone economic purpose means no defensible protection from anti-abuse rules.
insights.quote: If the wrapper serves no purpose other than to alter a tax outcome, the analysis should end there.

insights.claim: All conclusions about whether a wrapper avoids anti-abuse rules are void if the wrapper was created with specific intent to circumvent those rules, because broader anti-abuse doctrines override entity-level treatment.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Documenting non-tax intent matters; an audit trail showing tax-avoidance intent can collapse the whole structure regardless of technical partnership status.
insights.quote: the conclusions presented in the table are irrelevant if the wrapper was created with a specific intent to circumvent these rules

insights.claim: Wash sale rules (Section 1091) disallow a loss when a taxpayer sells securities and acquires substantially identical securities within 30 days before or after the sale, preventing loss harvesting while keeping continuous economic exposure.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Defines the 61-day window tax-loss harvesters must respect; central to any direct-indexing or tax-aware long/short program.

insights.claim: Of the three regimes, the straddle rules (Section 1092) have the broadest look-through: the taxpayer is expressly treated as holding positions held by related persons and by flowthrough entities including partnerships, regardless of the entity's independence.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: No partnership wrapper — not even a genuine commingled fund — protects against straddle-rule loss deferral; this is the regime where wrappers fail most universally.
insights.quote: straddle rules have one of the broadest look-through provisions. The taxpayer is expressly treated as holding positions held by related persons and by certain flowthrough entities.

insights.claim: Constructive sale rules (Section 1259) force gain recognition when an investor neutralizes substantive risk of loss and opportunity for gain via tight collars, shorts-against-the-box, or total return swaps.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Hedging an embedded-gain position too tightly triggers a taxable deemed sale; relevant to anyone using collars or TRS to defer gains.

insights.claim: Constructive sale rules generally apply if an investor forms a partnership and holds a majority interest to avoid those rules, even if avoidance was not the sole objective, but may not apply if the investor holds only a minority interest and merely incidentally holds offsetting trades elsewhere.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Interest size and intent are the swing variables for constructive-sale attribution; majority control plus offsetting positions is the danger zone.
insights.quote: Constructive sale rules would generally apply if the investor forms a partnership and holds a majority interest in such partnership to avoid the application of those rules, even if such avoidance was not the sole objective.

themes.theme: The entity spectrum and economic separation

insights.claim: A single-member LLC is typically disregarded as separate from its owner and an SMA is direct ownership with delegated discretion, so all wash sale, straddle, and constructive sale rules apply as if the investor transacted directly.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: SMAs and single-member LLCs offer zero anti-abuse protection — useful to know before assuming a managed account changes the tax calculus.

insights.claim: A fund-of-one is where the 'wrapper illusion' is most dangerous because it may technically qualify as a partnership but in substance resembles an SMA, and the sole investor's control — even informal control — collapses the distinction between investor and fund.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Funds-of-one used for tax arbitrage likely add audit risk without delivering protection; the single-investor control is the fatal weakness.
insights.quote: This is the structure where the wrapper illusion is most dangerous.

insights.claim: Minimal general-partner economics — little or no capital interest and no meaningful profit participation — do not automatically disqualify partnership status but weaken the narrative of genuine separateness the anti-abuse analysis requires.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Thin GP economics are a red flag examiners can use; structures with token GPs are easier to challenge.
insights.quote: While thin GP economics do not automatically disqualify partnership status, they do nothing to strengthen the narrative of genuine separateness that the anti-abuse analysis requires.

insights.claim: For a fund-of-one, straddle rules look through it regardless of partnership status, and it is almost certainly a 'related person' triggering full attribution under the constructive sale rules.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: A fund-of-one fails on two of the three regimes outright and invites scrutiny on the third (wash sales).
insights.quote: the fund-of-one is almost certainly a "related person" to the investor, triggering full attribution of positions
insights.locator: For wash sale purposes, a fund-of-one

themes.theme: Commingled funds — the only partial shield

insights.claim: A genuine multi-member fund earns legitimate economic separation only if it has pooled capital from multiple LPs, truly independent third-party management with full trading discretion, and no single investor able to direct individual trades.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: These three conditions are the test for whether a fund's partnership status will actually hold up against wash sale look-through.

insights.claim: In a genuine commingled fund, wash sale rules may not apply because the analysis starts at the entity level treating partner and partnership as separate, but straddle rules still expressly look through the partnership regardless of independence.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Even the best-case wrapper only helps on wash sales, never straddles — so a commingled fund is no escape hatch for offsetting-position loss deferral.
insights.quote: the partner and the partnership are technically treated as separate units, so wash sale rules may not apply because the analysis starts at the entity level. The straddle rules, however, expressly look through flowthrough entities
insights.locator: Under the wash sale rules, the partner

insights.claim: An investor in a genuine commingled fund typically does not hold a sufficient interest for the fund to qualify as a 'related person' under constructive sale rules, but anti-abuse principles can still apply where positions are coordinated across personal accounts and fund investments.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Coordination between personal and fund positions reintroduces anti-abuse exposure even in a commingled structure — keep the books genuinely separate.

insights.claim: The IRS has ruled that selling stock from a taxable account and rebuying the same shares through a retirement account the next day triggers the wash sale rules to disallow the loss, illustrating that account-type changes do not defeat wash sale attribution.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: Concrete precedent that cross-account wrappers fail; a cautionary data point for any harvesting program spanning account types.
insights.quote: the IRS has ruled that where a taxpayer sold stock from his taxable account and subsequently rebought the same shares through a retirement account the next day, the wash sale rules applied to disallow any losses
insights.locator: For wash sale purposes, a fund-of-one

insights.claim: AQR advises evaluating LPs and LLCs for what they genuinely provide — administrative efficiency, liability protection, operational flexibility — rather than as mechanisms to circumvent anti-abuse rules; if a structure's primary appeal is an economically unsupported tax outcome, especially if promoted as such, investors should stay away.
insights.speaker: AQR Perspectives
insights.so_what: A due-diligence rule of thumb: be wary of any structure marketed primarily on its tax-circumvention benefit.
insights.quote: If a structure's primary appeal is a tax outcome that the economics do not support—and, even worse, it is promoted as such—it is best to stay away.
